coredata.dc:title: One-pot green synthesis of in–situ functionalized carbon quantum dots from Agave americana for supercapacitor applications

coredata.dc:description: This research presents a sustainable energy storage approach through the environment-friendly green synthesis of functionalized fluorescence carbon quantum dots (CQDs), focusing on eco-friendly approaches that minimize environmental impact and enhance the scalability of production. In this research, a cost-effective, uncomplicated, and efficient environment-friendly one-pot hydrothermal synthesis process was employed for synthesizing highly fluorescent CQDs using Agave americana leaf fiber waste byproducts as novel natural green carbon precursors, devoid of any harmful chemicals for the first time. A variety of techniques were used to thoroughly characterize the synthesized CQDs, such as Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), zeta potential analysis, Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), Raman spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy, UV–visible spectroscopy, and X-ray Diffraction (XRD). The produced CQDs have a spherical morphology with low crystallinity and an average particle diameter of about 3.33 nm, as demonstrated by XRD and TEM investigations. Furthermore, it was found that the CQDs’ quantum yield (QY) was 40.42%. The CQDs exhibited favorable characteristics such as high fluorescence intensity, excitation-dependent emission properties, and excellent water solubility. Additionally, electrochemical characterizations were performed to assess the suitability of the synthesized CQDs for supercapacitor applications. The CQD electrode corroborated a specific capacitance of 90 F/g, demonstrating outstanding stability even after 1000 cycles.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Pitamber Mahanandia is currently serving as a Distinguished Professor in the Department of Physics & Astronomy at the National Institute of Technology (NIT) Rourkela, Odisha, India. He earned his Ph.D. in Physics from the Department of Physics at the Indian Institute of Science, Bangalore, India. Following his doctorate, he pursued postdoctoral research in the USA and Germany before joining NIT Rourkela. His research interests encompass carbon nanomaterials, quantum dots, field-effect transistors, quantum transport, sensors, photovoltaic cells, and energy storage devices. He leads the Functional Material research group at NIT Rourkela, focusing on the development and application of advanced materials in these areas. He has published numerous research articles in reputed national and international journals, with notable works such as \u201CSynthesis of multi-wall carbon nanotubes by simple pyrolysis,\u201D which has been cited over 70 times. In addition to his research contributions, he has been actively involved in academic events. Throughout his academic career, he has made significant contributions to the field of physics and materials science. In addition to his research endeavors, he has successfully led several sponsored projects funded by the Central Power Research Institute (CPRI), the Science and Engineering Research Board (SERB), and the Department of Science and Technology (DST). He has also demonstrated a strong commitment to mentoring the next generation of scientists, having supervised numerous doctoral students throughout his tenure. His dedication to both research and education continues to advance the fields of physics and materials science, fostering innovation and academic excellence at NIT Rourkela and beyond.

The National Institute of Technology in Rourkela, Odisha, India, has supported this research project. The XRD diffractometer (Rigaku, Ultima-IV) is partly supported by the Fund for Improvement of Science and Technology Infrastructure (FIST) grant (SR/FIST/PSI-156/2010, dated 28/12/2020) of the Department of Science & Technology (DST).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This research presents a sustainable energy storage approach through the environment-friendly green synthesis of functionalized fluorescence carbon quantum dots (CQDs), focusing on eco-friendly approaches that minimize environmental impact and enhance the scalability of production. In this research, a cost-effective, uncomplicated, and efficient environment-friendly one-pot hydrothermal synthesis process was employed for synthesizing highly fluorescent CQDs using Agave americana leaf fiber waste byproducts as novel natural green carbon precursors, devoid of any harmful chemicals for the first time. A variety of techniques were used to thoroughly characterize the synthesized CQDs, such as Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), zeta potential analysis, Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), Raman spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy, UV–visible spectroscopy, and X-ray Diffraction (XRD). The produced CQDs have a spherical morphology with low crystallinity and an average particle diameter of about 3.33 nm, as demonstrated by XRD and TEM investigations. Furthermore, it was found that the CQDs’ quantum yield (QY) was 40.42%. The CQDs exhibited favorable characteristics such as high fluorescence intensity, excitation-dependent emission properties, and excellent water solubility. Additionally, electrochemical characterizations were performed to assess the suitability of the synthesized CQDs for supercapacitor applications. The CQD electrode corroborated a specific capacitance of 90 F/g, demonstrating outstanding stability even after 1000 cycles.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), 0D carbon-based nanomaterials with dimensions less than 10 nm, have received considerable scientific interest as compared to traditional semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) [1] because of their outstanding physical and chemical properties, such as size-dependent optical properties, stable fluorescence behavior, excellent biocompatibility, low toxicity, excellent water solubility, tunable surface chemistry, low cost, and chemical inertness [2–5]. Because of their outstanding electronic and optical properties, CQDs are widely utilized in numerous applications, including biomedicine, sensing, energy conversion, different optoelectronic device applications, environmental remediation, etc. [6–9]. To date, CQDs have demonstrated promising as an electroactive material for energy storage applications, including solar cells, batteries, thermoelectric devices, and supercapacitors [10,11].

However, the physicochemical characteristics and versatile applications of CQDs are contingent upon both the carbon source materials utilized in their synthesis and the specific preparation techniques employed. Various approaches have been developed for the synthesis of CQDs, including bottom-up approaches such as hydrothermal carbonization [12,13], microwave-assisted pyrolysis [14], and laser ablation [15], as well as top-down techniques like chemical oxidation [16] and electrochemical exfoliation [17], etc. Additionally, the choice of carbon source materials to produce CQDs, such as biomass, inorganic compounds, organic compounds, or waste products, significantly influences the properties and performance of the resulting CQDs. These methods enable definite size control, morphology, surface chemistry, and optical properties of CQDs, allowing for tailored synthesis to meet specific application requirements. However, the methods mentioned earlier for synthesizing CQDs with high quantum yield (QY) face numerous constraints, including the employment of diverse hazardous chemicals as carbon sources, the utilization of hazardous solvents, poor yields, limited spectrum efficiency range of the final product, intricate post-treatment procedures, and high-energy consumption, limiting their large-scale production [18,19] and posing risks to the environment and human health [20,21].

In response, researchers have turned towards the green synthesis of CQDs, a sustainable approach towards the fabrication of the nanomaterials, emphasizing the use of renewable resources and non-toxic reagents, environmentally benign precursors, and sustainable, eco-friendly synthesis routes, which are increasingly being explored to address environmental concerns and to enhance the sustainability of CQDs production. Unlike traditional methods that often rely on toxic chemicals and non-renewable carbon sources, green synthesis techniques utilize biomass, natural extracts, or waste materials as carbon precursors and employ mild reaction conditions such as hydrothermal or microwave-assisted processes. These green synthesis routes not only reduce the environmental impact associated with CQDs production but also offer several advantages, such as scalability, high yield, low cost, and tunability of CQDs properties, making them suitable for large-scale production of CQDs for applications.[22,23] To date, a variety of sustainable natural resources, including marigold flowers [24], orange peels [25], rice husk [26], peanut skin [27], prawn shells [18], peanut shells [28], banana peel [29], watermelon peel [30], bamboo leaves [31], onion waste [32], coffee grounds [33], and Borassus flabillifier flowers [34], have already been employed as green natural renewable sources for the fluorescent CQDs synthesis extracted from biomass. A wide range of fluorescent CQDs have already been synthesized from a variety of green carbon precursors using various methods. However, many of the CQDs synthesized from these naturally abundant green sources often suffer from low fluorescence efficiency and QY, limiting their practical application. To address this, there is an urgent need for more efficient synthesis methods and green sources that can produce high QY and highly fluorescent CQDs on a large scale. The process of carbonization via hydrothermal heat is considered an environment-friendly and sustainable synthetic approach among the methods described above due to its convenience, efficiency, utilization of water solvent, and ability to yield CQDs having high quantum yield. Thus, driven by motivation, we have embraced a novel, eco-conscious synthetic route for producing CQDs, utilizing sustainable green raw materials as starting precursors.

In this context, here, in the current research investigation, we have developed an environmentally sustainable and eco-friendly synthesis approach to produce fluorescent CQDs. This method involves utilizing Agave americana leaf fibers waste byproducts as natural green precursors through a novel, simple, inexpensive, and economical hydrothermal carbonization process devoid of any hazardous substances and surface passivating agents. Agave americana, commonly known as the century plant, locally named as murga in the Sundergarh district of Odisha, India, is a succulent plant that represents an abundant and renewable biomass source that holds promise for the green synthesis of CQDs [35]. Native to Mexico and widely cultivated in various regions worldwide as an ornamental plant, Agave americana is known for its versatile applications in the construction of ropes for agriculture, food, handicrafts, medicinal purposes, beverages, and fiber productions [36,37]. The leaves of Agave americana are rich in cellulose, hemicellulose, wax, and lignin, which may be useful as renewable green sources for synthesizing CQDs. Furthermore, from ancient times, the fiber extracted from the Agave americana leaf was known as pita, which was widely used in the making of doormats, sandals, bracelets, bags, carpets, nets, and leather embroidery by utilizing the method called piteado due to the outstanding properties of it including low density and high tensile strength [37,38]. Usually, the waste by-products generated after the processing of Agave americana fibers are discarded as bio-degradable organic waste to the land. Therefore, we have used this waste byproduct of Agave americana leaf fibers as a sustainable green precursor for synthesizing CQDs without the use of any harmful solvents. To the best of our knowledge, Agave americana leaf fiber waste byproducts have never been used as a carbon precursor for the synthesis of CQDs. The properties of the as-synthesized CQDs have been investigated by using a variety of analytical techniques, including Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), zeta potential analysis, Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), Raman spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy, UV–Visible spectroscopy, and X-ray Diffraction (XRD) techniques. The CQDs synthesized from Agave americana leaf fiber waste by-products exhibit unique properties. This approach allows for the large-scale production of high-quality CQDs with an average particle size of 3.33 nm, smaller than those reported in previous studies [39], and a high QY of 40.42%, larger than earlier work [39,40]. The synthesized CQDs exhibited excellent fluorescence properties and were rich in various functional groups, all without the need for any chemical passivating agents. Therefore, in order to find the effectiveness of the synthesized CQDs by the green method, the CQDs were employed as electrode material for applications in supercapacitors. A few works of literature have already been reported based on the supercapacitor applications of CQDs [41,42]. Prasath et al. [42] utilized CQDs-MnO2 nanohybrid, synthesized using precipitation methods, as an electrode material for supercapacitor applications. Zhu et al. [43] employed CQDs, synthesized using chemical oxidation methods, as an electrode material for supercapacitor applications. However, the earlier reported work requires harmful chemicals for the synthesis of CQD electrodes, which poses a risk to the environment. Also, the earlier resulting CQD electrode demonstrates a lower specific capacitance value, which limits its practical applications. Therefore, in the quest for sustainable energy solutions and eco-friendly synthesis routes, the researchers aim to develop CQD-based electrode materials by using green synthesis methods. It has been observed that at a current density of 1 A/g, the CQD electrode synthesized by using this green synthetic route demonstrated a specific capacitance of 90 F/g; this value correlates favorably withthe previouslyreported [10].

Agave americana leaf fibers were obtained from Sundergarh, Odisha, India. Nayak et al. [35] have already identified in their report and investigated Agave americana, identifying the availability in the Sundergarh district, Odisha, India. This plant is commonly available in this district and somewhere in the open field as well, as in some places like other plants or trees. So, the identification of the plant has been taken from reference 35. In this work, only the waste has been used, which is usually dumped after the fiber is taken out. All of the chemical reagents, including H2SO4, carbon black, ethanol, and Nafion, were procured from Merck, India. Analytical-grade chemicals were utilized throughout the experiment without any additional purification. Milli-Q water was utilized for preparing all aqueous solutions in the experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CQDs from Agave americana leaf fibers
ce:section.ce:para: CQDs were synthesized from green precursor Agave americana leaf fibers waste byproducts employing an easy one-pot hydrothermal carbonization process. The waste byproducts from Agave americana leaf fiber have been extracted by using a simple method. First, the leaves of Agave americana were dried outside in the sun, broken down into smaller pieces, fully cleaned using a solution of ethanol, acetone, and Milli-Q water, and then allowed to dry outside in the sun. Then, the broken pieces of leaf fibers were ground by using a mixer grinder, and the resulting powdered waste byproducts were taken out after the extraction of fibers for further processing. The biomass powder was thoroughly washed using a solution of acetone and Milli-Q water, and finally, it was dried in an oven set to 70 °C for an additional 10 h. Then, 4 g of biomass of leaf fibers were taken and added with 70 mL of Milli-Q water, after which, for 40 min, it was subjected to ultrasonic treatment until the color of Milli-Q water turned yellowish brown. Following that, the solution mixture was shifted to a 100 mL autoclave with Teflon-lined from the beaker and subjected to heating at 200 °C for 10 h in the muffle furnace. Upon reaching room temperature, the dark orange solution was collected, after which any unreacted organic components and large particles were separated through centrifugation at 12,000 rpm for 30 min. Fluorescent CQDs were obtained, following filtering using the 0.22 μm syringe filter and dialyzing against Milli-Q water using a dialysis membrane for 24 h to eliminate low-molecular-weight organic impurities and salt residues. The resulting purified CQDs were subsequently stored inside a refrigerator maintained at 4 °C for additional testing and experimentation. An illustration of the CQD synthesis scheme from waste byproducts of the Agave americana leaf fibers is shown in Fig. 1 .

In order to investigate molecular structure and the chemical bonding of CQDs, Raman spectra have been studied through the Raman spectrophotometer equipped with a solid laser beam (WITec Wissenschaftliche Instrumente und Technologie GmbH Model-XMB3000-3000) emitting light at 633 nm wavelength and the range of scanning of power of 400–3000 /cm. The synthesized CQDs' The absorption spectrum of the synthesized CQDs was captured utilizing a UV–vis spectrometer (Shimadzu/UV-1800), with absorbance spectra recorded within the wavelength range of 200 nm − 800 nm. Surface functional groups of the as-prepared CQDs have been examined through a Fourier-transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR, Bruker, Alpha E). To assess the crystalline nature of the CQDs, XRD patterns have been recorded utilizing a multifunctional X-ray Diffractometer instrument (JAPAN, RIGAKU, & THE ULTIMA −IV) at 2θ values ranging between 20˚ to 70˚. Utilizing the fluorescence spectrometer instrument (Jobin Yvon Fluoromax-4P), the CQDs' fluorescence spectrum was acquired. Morphological analysis of the prepared CQDs was performed using a TEM instrument (FEI, Tecnai G2 TF30-ST). The zeta potential was determined using the instrument zeta potential analyzer (ZS90, Malvern Zeta Sizer Nano). For each measurement, a transparent reusable zeta cell filled with 1 mL of CQDs sample, which was well-dispersed, was taken. All the measurements have been conducted at normal room temperature to determine the zeta potential value. The elemental composition and surface functional groups of the synthesized CQDs were studied using the X-ray Photo-Electron Spectroscopy (XPS) Beamline (BL-14) under ultrahigh vacuum conditions at the Indus-2 synchrotron in RRCAT Indore, India.

A previously established method was employed to determine the quantum yield of the synthesized CQDs (ΦCQD) [44]. Quinine sulfate (QS) was prepared as the reference, and five separate concentrations of the sampleCQDswere also prepared. Each concentration was tested at 330 nm excitation wavelength, making sure that each sample maintained an absorbance measurement under 0.10 to reduce the re-absorption effects. QS was dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4 (with a QY of 54% and an index of refraction (ɳ) of 1.33) to make the reference solution. The integrated fluorescence intensities and absorbance valuesof the sample and reference solutions were compared at the same excitation wavelengthin order to determine the QY of the CQDs sample. Using the equation (2) provided below, the quantum yield (ΦCQD) of the prepared CQDs was evaluated:
(1)

In the given context, Φ denotes the QY, with subscripts R and CQD indicating the reference and sample, respectively. I represent the integrated fluorescence emission intensity, while A corresponds to absorbance. The solvent medium refractive index is represented by η, and the variable m signifies the slope.

The synthesized CQDs electrochemical properties were studied by employing an electrochemical workstation (Corrtest, China) instrument. This instrument was utilized to evaluate the supercapacitor properties of the prepared materials using galvanostatic charging-discharging (GCD), Cyclic voltammogram (CV), and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) analysis. The electrochemical measurements of the prepared CQDs were conducted using a three-electrode cell configuration comprising platinum (Pt) wire acting as the counter electrode, the CQDs employing as the working electrode, and Ag/AgCl acting as the reference electrode. For the concoction of the working electrode, a homogeneous slurry was prepared by thoroughly mixing the carbon black (20 wt%) to enhance conductivity as active material CQDs (70 wt%) and Nafion as a binder (10 wt%) using ethanol as a solvent. The resulting slurry was then loaded on the top of the graphite rod polished surface and dried up by using the oven maintained at 70 °C for 24 h. The electrode material loading mass coated onto the graphite rod was determined to be 0.2 mg. The GCD, CV, and EIS analyses were performed with the help of three-electrode-containing cells in 1 M H2SO4 electrolyte. EIS measurement was carried out across a frequency range of 0.01 Hz to 100 kHz with a 10 mV alternating current (AC) signal. Every electrochemical test has been conducted at normal room temperature. The electrode material's specific capacitance (C) value has been estimated from the curve of GCD by employing the formula in equation (3) given below.
(3)
C =
I × Δ t
Δ v × m
whereas I represent the constant current applied, m denotes the loading mass,
Δ v
define the window potential and
Δ t
represent the discharging time.

TEM characterization was employed to examine the surface morphology and average particle size of the CQDs. Fig. 2 a illustrates that CQDs exhibited uniform dispersion, appearing spherical with 2 to 5 nm diameters. The produced CQDs possessed small crystalline structures, as indicated by the TEM image. The histogram distribution of particle size depicted in Fig. 2 b, derived from the analysis of several hundred particles, revealed an average diameter of approximately 3.33 nm; this value is smaller as compared to the previously reported article [45,46]. To study the elemental composition of the synthesized CQDs, Energy Dispersive X-ray Spectroscopy (EDS) analysis was performed. The EDS spectrum (Fig. 2 c) showed that the sample primarily contains 68.38 wt% of C and 8.18 wt% of N.

Two prominent broad peaks are observed in the XRD pattern of the synthesized CQDs, as illustrated in Fig. 3 a: first appearing around 2θ = 24.7° which corresponds to the (002), and the second at around 43.2° attributed to (100) graphitic lattice planes exhibiting sp2 hybridization. The observed broad diffraction pattern of XRD can be attributed to the crystalline carbon atoms with oxygen-containing groups, which are closely spaced [47].

The presence of graphitic carbon was verified through an analysis of Raman spectroscopy. Two recognizable peaks at nearly 1361 cm−1 and 1576 cm−1 are displayed by the Raman spectra as depicted in Fig. 3 b, correspondent to the D band indicating the presence of defects and the G band, which is associated with the sp2-conjugated carbon structure, respectively [48]. The peak intensity ratio of the D disordered band peak intensity (ID) and the G crystalline band peak intensity (IG) has been calculated to assess the structural integrity of the synthesized CQDs with respect to graphite and was determined to be 0.94. This suggests that the as-prepared CQDs are mostly composed of graphitic carbon clusters exhibiting excellent features [49].

The surface charge (zeta potential) of the prepared CQDs has been investigated through the measurements of zeta potential and discovered to be −8.21 mV, as depicted in Fig. 4 . The negative charge possessed by the prepared CQDs has been confirmed from these findings, potentially attributed to the functional groups that contain oxygen upon the CQDs surfaces.

FTIR spectroscopy measurement was conducted in an effort to interpret the different surface functional groups existing on the as-prepared CQDs, as depicted in Fig. 5 . The strong absorption band at 3318 cm−1 corresponded to the asymmetric O–H stretching vibration mode of the hydroxyl groups and the N–H stretching vibration bond of the secondary amine group. The distinct and precise peak observed at 1634 cm−1 indicates the bending vibration of amide (N–H), which corroborates the incorporation of nitrogen without any addition of external nitrogen source and the carbon double bonds (C=C) stretching vibration mode or −C=O vibration bond of stretching mode [50]. The weak absorption band observed at 2091 cm−1 may refer to the presence of a nitrile group (–CN). The small absorption bands at 1360 cm−1 may be appropriated to the C–H bond bending vibration mode. Based on these findings, it is inferred that the synthesized CQDs predominantly consist of sp2 hybridized carbon, featuring hydroxyl, carboxyl, nitrile, carbonyl, amino, and methyl functional groups on their surfaces. The FTIR analysis indicates that the CQDs exhibit hydrophilic properties, rendering them more stable and easily dispersible in water because of the abundance of functional groups present on their surfaces. No additional processing was conducted to functionalize the synthesized CQDs [51].

XPS analysis was carried out to further validate the surface functional groups and chemical compositions of the as-prepared CQDs. The XPS survey spectrum profile of the CQDs (Fig. 6 a) confirmed that mainly C, N, and O elements are present in the sample CQDs. The wide scan survey of the XPS spectrum (Fig. 6 a) revealed three distinct peaks attributed to C1s, N1s, and O1s at a binding energy of 285.5 eV, 397.4 eV, and 530.8 eV, respectively. The high-resolution XPS scan of the C1s spectrum (Fig. 6 b) was deconvoluted into three distinct peaks attributed to 284.5 eV (C–C/C=C), 285.7 eV (C-O/C-N), and 287.9 eV (C=O), respectively [52], while the high-resolution O1s spectrum (Fig. 6 c) revealed three distinct peaks corresponding to 531.4 eV (N-O/C-OH), 533 eV (O=C), and 535.1 eV (O-C), respectively, confirming the presence of diverse oxygenated functional groups [53,54]. The high-resolution XPS scan of the N1s spectrum (Fig. 6 d) showed two peaks ascribed to 398.7 eV (amide/amine) and 399.3 eV (pyrrolic nitrogen), respectively [55,56].

A variety of oxygen and nitrogen-containing functional groups, which contain sp2 and sp3 hybridized carbon atoms, are present on the surface of the CQDs, as evidenced by the FTIR and XPS results, which impart excellent hydrophilicity to the synthesized CQDs. These functional groups also introduce defect sites and emissive trap states on the surface of the CQDs, which influence electron-hole recombination dynamics and significantly influence the fluorescence properties of CQDs [57–59]. Based on the analysis, it is evident that the CQDs possess electron-donating amino groups (–NH2), which are covered by O–H bonds, which are weakly electron-withdrawing carboxyl (–COOH) groups, indicating the presence of moderately electron-withdrawing surface functionalities [53]. Notably, XPS data further reveal a relatively high content of carbonyl (C=O) groups, suggesting a dominance of electron-withdrawing carboxyl functionalities over electron-donating ones. This increased presence of electron-withdrawing groups in CQDs ultimately induces a redshift in the excitation-dependent fluorescence properties of the CQDs [56].

Three prominent absorption peaks were observed from the UV–Vis spectra study of the prepared CQDs, as portrayed in Fig. 7 a. The electronic transition of the π-π* attributed to the C=C bonds is responsible for two peaks that are detected around 249 nm and 278 nm. On the other hand, the electronic transition of n-π* ascribed to C=O aromatic rings is responsible for the third peak, which is observed around 323 nm [60].

Using fluorescence spectroscopy, the fluorescent features of the as-prepared CQDs have been investigated, as demonstrated in Fig. 7 b. The solution of CQDs appeared pale yellow color in natural light, but a vivid blue fluorescent emission peak is observed at a wavelength of 412 nm in the fluorescence spectra when it is excited at 330 nm wavelength. Additional research on the fluorescence characteristics reveals that CQDs exhibited the distinctive behavior of emission intensity that depends on the excitation intensity, as illustrated in Fig. 7 c [61]. Different emission intensities were observed at various excitation wavelength ranges from about 300 to 380 nm, along with an ongoing 10 nm interspace. With an increase in excitation wavelength, it is noticed that the emission intensity is red-shifted, having the maximum emission intensity peak at 330 nm wavelength of excitation. This demonstrates that by adjusting the excitation wavelength, synthesized CQDs can display multicolored (blue, green, and red) emission [62]. These fluorescent characteristics of the synthesized CQDs may be elucidated by factors such as the emissive traps, aromatic conjugate structures, giant red edge effect, and the availability of a variety of functional groups on the surfaces, as already corroborated from FTIR spectra [63].

Further investigations were done on the CQDs' photostability and stability, two crucial characteristics for practical applications. Fig. 8 a displays the fluorescence emission spectra of the CQD solutions at different periods of storage intervals. CQD fluorescence intensity is almost the same at the start of synthesis and after 90 days of storage. These small intensity changes confirm the long-term stability of CQDs. The results of testing the photostability of CQDs under 365 nm UV lamp radiation for 75 min are displayed in Fig. 8 b. Remarkably, the irradiated CQDs showed only minimal differences in fluorescence intensity compared to non-irradiated CQDs, confirming their exceptional photostability [64]. This observed stability can be primarily attributed to the protective effect of surface defect states and the presence of abundant oxygen- and nitrogen-containing functional groups on the surface of CQDs. These functional groups play a crucial role in passivating surface trap states and preventing photo-induced degradation by stabilizing the photo-excited states. Furthermore, surface defect sites often formed during low-temperature green synthesis can serve as stabilizing centers that protect the carbon core from UV-induced structural damage. The intrinsic stability of the carbon core, along with its π-conjugated domains, also contributes to enhancing the overall optical durability of the CQDs under prolonged UV exposure [65].

We also determined the quantum yield demonstrated by CQDs using QS as a reference standard. As shown in Fig. 7 d, the integrated fluorescence intensity and absorbancevalues recorded at the wavelength of excitation of 330 nm for the reference (QS) and the sample (CQDs) were plotted, and the corresponding slopes were computed. Excellent linearity was demonstrated by the confirmed data, which had an intercept value around zero. Using these values, the QY of the CQDs synthesized from Agave americana fiber waste was estimated and found to be 40.42%. In addition to the relative standard method, we have cross-validated our results using the absolute method with an integrating sphere setup, and the calculated QY of CQDs was found to be nearly equal to 40.42% under the same excitation wavelength of 330 nm [56,66]. This result clearly indicates that the QY of CQDs prepared via hydrothermal method using Agave americana fiber waste is 40.42%. In comparison, this value is larger than many previous studies, particularly those utilizing other green sources [67,68].

CV measurement was conducted to assess the electrochemical behavior of the CQDs. As depicted in Fig. 9 a, the CV curves recorded in 1 M H2SO4 of the CQD electrode were within the range of potential from −0.3–0.3 V vs. Ag/AgCl at various scan rates. The characteristic rectangular shape of the CV curves indicates the presence of double-layer capacitance [69]. Furthermore, the CV current gradually increases with a gradual increase in the scan rate. The electrode material demonstrates remarkable electrochemical stability and ideal capacitive behavior, as evidenced by the smooth, evenat larger scan rates, CV curves are rectangular shapes. At different current densities, GCD curves were obtained, as illustrated in Fig. 9 b, revealing nearly symmetrical charge/discharge curves across all currents, indicative of a perfect double-layer capacitance. It can be observed that the CQD electrode exhibits a 90 F/g specific capacitance at a 1 A/g current density. The estimated specific capacitance value of the CQDs electrode at different current densities is depicted in Fig. 9 c. Table 1 presents a comparative analysis of the specific capacitance values of CQDs synthesized from Agave americana fiber waste with other green synthesized CQDs. The results indicate that the Agave-derived CQD-based supercapacitor electrode exhibits comparable to or even better specific capacitance compared to those fabricated from other natural biomass sources reported in previous literature, with the exception of CQD electrodes derived from milk, which show slightly higher values. These variations in specific capacitance values can be attributed to variations in experimental parameters such as the applied current density, scan rates, and the type of electrolyte used during electrochemical testing [70]. Furthermore, on the density of current at 1 A/g, the cycle stability of the manufactured CQD electrode material was examined over multiple charge/discharge cycles. The resulting data, presented in Fig. 9 d, show that even after 1000 cycles, during the charging and discharging processes, there was no significant capacitive behavior change, demonstrating excellent long-time cycle stability.

The presence of equivalent series resistances and charge transfer resistance in the fabricated electrode was investigated using EIS. EIS measurement was carried out across a frequency range of 0.01 Hz to 100 kHz with a 10 mV AC signal to study the electrochemical performances of the fabricated CQDs electrodes. The EIS data were fitted using a modified Randles equivalent electrical circuit, as shown in the inset of Fig. 10 . In this circuit, Rs represents the electrolyte resistance, Rct is the charge transfer resistance, Cdl denotes the electric double-layer capacitance, and Zw corresponds to the Warburg impedance. Fig. 10 demonstrates the Nyquist plot for the EIS characterization, and the plot reveals the absence of a semicircle, suggesting the presence of good electrical conductivity in the electrode or low charge transfer resistance between electrolytes and the electrode surface. Additionally, the vertical line observed in the low-frequency region indicates improved diffusion of electrolyte ions to the CQDs electrode and superior capacitive behavior [72]. The equivalent series resistance (ESR) of the CQDs electrode was calculated by determining the intercept at the Z'-axis and found to be 1.03 Ω, indicating very low resistance and better energy storage performance in CQD-based supercapacitors [73,74].

In summary, in this study, we have established a straightforward, cost-effective, environmentally friendly, and sustainable green synthesis approach to produce bright blue fluorescence CQDs utilizing Agave americana leaf fiber waste byproducts as the novel natural green precursors through facile one-step hydrothermal carbonization method, devoid of any hazardous chemical reagents and surface passivating agents. Using the FTIR spectroscopy method, the existence of various surface functional groups, including hydroxyl, carboxyl, carbonyl, and nitrile groups, on the as-prepared CQDs were investigated. Raman spectroscopy was utilized to identify the chemical structural bonding within the CQDs. The as-prepared CQDs exhibited a spherical morphology with an average diameter of particle size of approximately 3.33 nm and displayed crystalline properties revealed from TEM and XRD analysis. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) spectroscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy are employed to study their optical properties, including absorption and emission spectra. Furthermore, the green-synthesized CQDs carried a negative charge, and their QY was corroborated to be 40.42%. The synthesized CQDs exhibited favorable characteristics such as excellent water solubility, excitation-dependent emission intensity properties, and robust fluorescence behavior. These advantageous properties, combined with the simplicity of their synthesis methods, impressive fluorescence characteristics, minimal cytotoxicity, and cost-effectiveness, make them highly versatile for applications in optoelectronics, energy storage devices, and biological fields. We have successfully employed the synthesized CQDs to develop high-performance supercapacitor electrodes. The CQD electrode exhibited high-performance supercapacitor properties with a value of 90 F/g specific capacitance and outstanding stability over 1000 cycles. The results demonstrate promising electrochemical performance, highlighting the potential of these green synthesized CQDs as sustainable and promising materials for energy storage devices.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Santosini Patra is a dedicated research scholar specializing in carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and their applications in nanotechnology. She is currently pursuing her Ph.D. in Physics at the Department of Physics & Astronomy of the National Institute of Technology (NIT) Rourkela while serving as an Assistant Professor in Physics at the Government Autonomous College, Rourkela, Odisha, India. Her work primarily focuses on the green synthesis, characterization, and functional applications of CQDs in fields such as energy storage, fluorescence sensing, corrosion science, and environmental applications. She obtained her M.Sc. degree from the NIT Rourkela, where she developed a strong foundation in materials science and nanotechnology. She has successfully qualified for GATE and NET, demonstrating her academic excellence and research aptitude. With a keen interest in advancing sustainable nanomaterials, she has contributed to the field through notable publications in reputed journals, focusing on eco-friendly synthesis methods and innovative applications of CQDs. In addition to her research, she has gained two years of teaching experience at the Higher Education Department, Government of Odisha, mentoring undergraduate and postgraduate students. Her dedication to research and teaching underscores her commitment to advancing scientific knowledge and education in her field.

Pitamber Mahanandia is currently serving as a Distinguished Professor in the Department of Physics & Astronomy at the National Institute of Technology (NIT) Rourkela, Odisha, India. He earned his Ph.D. in Physics from the Department of Physics at the Indian Institute of Science, Bangalore, India. Following his doctorate, he pursued postdoctoral research in the USA and Germany before joining NIT Rourkela. His research interests encompass carbon nanomaterials, quantum dots, field-effect transistors, quantum transport, sensors, photovoltaic cells, and energy storage devices. He leads the Functional Material research group at NIT Rourkela, focusing on the development and application of advanced materials in these areas. He has published numerous research articles in reputed national and international journals, with notable works such as “Synthesis of multi-wall carbon nanotubes by simple pyrolysis,” which has been cited over 70 times. In addition to his research contributions, he has been actively involved in academic events. Throughout his academic career, he has made significant contributions to the field of physics and materials science. In addition to his research endeavors, he has successfully led several sponsored projects funded by the Central Power Research Institute (CPRI), the Science and Engineering Research Board (SERB), and the Department of Science and Technology (DST). He has also demonstrated a strong commitment to mentoring the next generation of scientists, having supervised numerous doctoral students throughout his tenure. His dedication to both research and education continues to advance the fields of physics and materials science, fostering innovation and academic excellence at NIT Rourkela and beyond.

Manoranjan Mandal is currently serving as an Assistant Professor at GITAM University, Bengaluru, India. He completed his Ph.D. in Physics from the Department of Physics & Astronomy at the National Institute of Technology (NIT) Rourkela, where his research focused on energy storage devices, specifically supercapacitors, batteries, and solar cells. His work primarily involves the synthesis and fabrication of carbon-based hybrid electrodes for energy storage applications. He has published numerous research articles in reputed national and international journals with notable works in his field. With extensive experience in the development of advanced materials and devices, his research aims to enhance the performance and efficiency of energy storage systems, contributing to sustainable energy solutions.
